full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.00|> So if any of you have tried to email me in the past two weeks, you've gotten an out-of-office message.<|8.00|> <|9.00|> I had the privilege for the third summer in a row of serving the most exquisite summer chapel on Southport Island in Maine.<|20.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It sits right on the rocky coastline, and some folks even come to church by boat.<|7.60|> <|7.60|> And it looks for all the world like something straight out of a movie.<|13.88|> <|13.88|> So my husband and the dogs and I reveled in a working vacation and returned on Friday night.<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So much like we do here at St. John's in the summertime, there is a weekday morning<|8.10|> <|8.10|> lectionary study. And I might say that coffee and conversation about Scripture,<|15.00|> <|15.00|> on the porch of all saints by the sea, with the waves lapping just a few feet away,<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> slightly more inspirational than doing it in room 107,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> but just slightly, just slightly.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> So this past Tuesday morning,<|10.00|> <|10.00|> we had one of the most raw and real conversations<|14.00|> <|14.00|> I have ever had with parishioners<|18.00|> <|18.00|> about any passage of scripture.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> And I know that my style of speaking<|24.00|> <|24.00|> can sometimes be hyperbolic,<|26.00|> <|26.00|> but I am not exaggerating.<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This text from James struck a chord.<|6.50|> <|6.50|> Perhaps it did so deeply because it was Tuesday morning and we were all anticipating later that night a certain debate on television.<|19.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.50|> I know that we are all weary, all of us,<|5.78|> <|5.78|> of the interminable election cycle<|8.72|> <|8.72|> and the inflamed rhetoric on all sides<|12.16|> <|12.16|> of present day politics.<|13.74|> <|14.88|> And I also know that for this particular group of folks<|19.16|> <|19.16|> on that porch, the metaphors that James uses<|23.30|> <|23.30|> resonated deeply.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I mean, this was a crowd of sailors who know all too well that a tiny rudder can, in fact,<|9.12|> <|9.12|> steer an enormous ship.<|12.40|> <|12.40|> And they are also people who live on an island surrounded by seawater and for whom freshwater<|18.88|> <|18.88|> is precious.<|20.04|> <|20.04|> And every one of them could answer James' question.<|23.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> does a spring pour forth from the same opening both fresh and brackish water.<|6.80|> <|8.88|> But you don't have to be on the coast of Maine for this scripture to speak loudly to us, do we?<|19.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.74|> It's the paradox of our ability, our ability,<|7.24|> <|7.24|> to speak both blessing and curse with our tongues.<|12.08|> <|13.28|> I too, individuals come up to me after that discussion<|17.76|> <|17.76|> and share how even in their very mature years,<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> they still struggled with their own sense of belovedness<|6.00|> <|6.00|> because of horribly cruel things that had been said to them in their lifetimes<|11.94|> <|11.94|> by family, friends, or even the church.<|16.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They had been wounded deeply by words, just as surely as if the tongue had been a sword.<|9.24|> <|9.24|> Indeed, as observed by Washington Irving, the tongue is the only tool that gets sharper<|16.18|> <|16.18|> with use.<|17.18|> <|17.18|> It is a weapon that can do incredible, life-long damage.<|25.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> And we know this, don't we?<|3.00|> <|3.00|> And yet, we still struggle to tame it.<|8.00|> <|9.00|> There have been many passages over the past month in our lectionary that have sort of tied into this very theme.<|18.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And our message from today's epistle fits perfectly with Richard's reflection a few weeks ago on wisdom.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> It was awesome. He told that great story about his daughter's kindergarten teacher, Mrs. Probst,<|15.00|> <|15.00|> who said, "Just because you think it, doesn't mean you have to say it."<|20.00|> <|20.00|> I don't know about y'all, but I've been pondering that over the last few weeks.<|25.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> especially on a two day drive from Maine to Colorado with my husband.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> Yet two days. 18 hours one day, 14 the next.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> There were many opportunities to hold my tongue.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> And he his. I'm just being fair.<|17.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We have had, over these weeks, the lessons of wisdom.<|7.88|> <|9.52|> And tonight, we meet wisdom's partner, accountability.<|14.88|> <|14.88|> Accountability.<|15.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was just two weeks ago that we heard Jesus say in Mark's gospel, listen to me, all of you, and understand there is nothing outside of a person that by going in can defile.<|15.28|> <|16.08|> But the things that come out are what defile.<|20.70|> <|20.70|> Amen.<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Because it's within our hearts and it's within our minds that those evil thoughts and feelings begin.<|9.30|> <|11.72|> The things that come out of us, out of our mouths, are what defile.<|19.26|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.04|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's interesting in light of that to think about how anatomically our tongues sit right<|7.74|> <|7.74|> between our hearts and our minds.<|11.14|> <|11.14|> From the same mouth comes blessing and cursing.<|15.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This wisdom has been colloquialized in pop culture, from Austin Powers to Spongebob, from the Avengers to Wayne's World with the line,<|10.44|> <|11.26|> Do you kiss your mother with that mouth?<|13.80|> <|15.32|> From the same mouth come blessing and cursing.<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Many consider James to <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And as with most epistles, James is writing to a community that is trying to figure out how to live faithfully as followers of Jesus.<|13.38|> <|14.90|> Just like we are.<|16.52|> <|17.98|> And just like we do, they struggled.<|21.26|> <|21.26|> They struggled.<|22.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My friends, we are accountable for the things that issue forth from our mouths, accountable for that tongue which can both bless and curse.<|16.40|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.92|> And what a timely reminder this is.<|4.36|> <|4.36|> These days of division in our culture,<|7.20|> <|7.20|> these days when it has never been easier<|10.14|> <|10.14|> to verbally assault one another.<|12.58|> <|13.52|> The letter writer could never have imagined<|17.86|> <|17.86|> when they penned these words,<|20.04|> <|20.04|> the great forest that could be set ablaze by the internet.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Lord, have mercy.<|3.26|> <|5.76|> So what hope do we have?<|7.26|> <|8.32|> It's kind of doom and gloomy, isn't it?<|10.00|> <|13.22|> I do think we have hope.<|14.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yeah.<|1.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We have a reminder to be aware and we have an exhortation to be accountable.<|10.44|> <|10.44|> Awareness and accountability.<|14.16|> <|14.16|> James assures us that all of us make mistakes.<|18.04|> <|18.04|> Perfection is not the goal.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And the tongue is virtually incapable of being tamed.<|8.16|> <|8.16|> Such is the human condition.<|11.82|> <|11.82|> Our baptismal covenant talks about when we fall into sin.<|17.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> not if, when. It's an assumption that it will happen. We will make mistakes. So in<|10.44|> <|10.44|> light of that, what do we do? We just say, "Oh well, I can't get any better." No.<|17.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We remember to bless.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> We remember to praise God and speak love to one another.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> We remember that the same tongue which sings and prays and praises<|18.00|> <|18.00|> should love its neighbor who is made in the likeness of God.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And when we fail, which we will, and when we make mistakes, let me hear you say, which we will.<|8.98|> <|9.44|> When we make mistakes, oh, amen.<|13.50|> <|14.50|> We take responsibility for it.<|17.58|> <|18.34|> We ask forgiveness from God and from our neighbor.<|23.46|> <|23.46|> And we try again and again and again.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Remember to bless.<|3.32|> <|6.54|> Jesus asked the disciples in the gospel lesson<|10.60|> <|10.60|> that I don't know that you even heard<|12.06|> <|12.06|> because Zane and I were making such a distraction<|14.26|> <|14.26|> passing out papers.<|15.20|> <|15.74|> I'm sorry, Amanda.<|16.40|> <|18.06|> But Jesus asked the disciples in that gospel lesson tonight<|21.40|> <|21.40|> to say,<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Who do people say that I am?<|4.88|> <|4.88|> And I would ask you a similar, more intimate, close to home question.<|11.06|> <|11.06|> Who do people say that you are?<|16.76|> <|16.76|> Who do people say that St. John's is?<|21.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For my friends, like it or not, we are not, well, we're rarely known by our intentions.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> We're not just known by our actions. We are also known by our words.<|16.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and may our words of blessing flow<|4.26|> <|4.26|> so that we may know how to sustain the weary with a word.<|10.28|> <|11.48|> Amen.<|12.04|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>
